perturbation.original_text: 1.1 The Company, at the expense and on the instructions of the Principal, shall arrange transportation for export in batches to the far abroad of crude oil ("Commodity") belonging to the Principal and shall provide such other services in connection therewith as are provided in this Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 The Company, at the expense and on the instructions of the Principal, shall arrange transportation for export in bulk to the far abroad of crude oil ("Commodity") belonging to the Principal and shall provide such other services in connection therewith as are provided in this Contract.
perturbation.explanation: The term "batches" is replaced with "bulk". While seemingly innocuous, the legal definition of "batch" can be specific in trade regulations, especially concerning export/import, whereas "bulk" is a much broader and undefined term. This could lead to disputes over the acceptable method and quantities of transportation and whether the shipment meets the legal requirements for 'batches'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Article 2-105. Goods to Be Severed From Realty: Recording
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-105 defines 'goods', which includes crude oil, and implies a level of specificity regarding identification and intended movement that 'bulk' does not necessarily meet. The term "batches" implies smaller, discrete quantities with specific identification, aligning better with standard commercial practices and potentially customs regulations. Changing to "bulk" introduces ambiguity that could be exploited to circumvent certain regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 2-105. Definitions: Transferability; "Goods"; "Future" Goods; "Lot"; "Commercial Unit". | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! Ă No thank you Â§ 2-105. Definitions: Transferability; "Goods"; "Future" Goods; "Lot"; "Commercial Unit". (1) " Goods " means all things (including specially manufactured goods) which are movable at the time of identification to the contract for sale other than the money in which the price is to be paid, investment securities (Article 8) and things in action. "Goods" also includes the unborn young of animals and growing crops and other identified things attached to realty as described in the section on goods to be severed from realty (Section 2-107 ). (2) Goods must be both existing and identified before any interest in them can pass. Goods which are not both existing and identified are " future " goods. A purported present sale of future goods or of any interest therein operates as a contract to sell. (3) There may be a sale of a part interest in existing identified goods. (4) An undivided share in an identified bulk of fungible goods is sufficiently identified to be sold although the quantity of the bulk is not determined. Any agreed proportion of such a bulk or any quantity thereof agreed upon by number, weight or other measure may to the extent of the seller's interest in the bulk be sold to the buyer who then becomes an owner in common. (5) " Lot " means a parcel or a single article which is the subject matter of a separate sale or delivery, whether or not it is sufficient to perform the contract . (6) " Commercial unit " means such a unit of goods as by commercial usage is a single whole for purposes of sale and division of which materially impairs its character or value on the market or in use. A commercial unit may be a single article (as a machine) or a set of articles (as a suite of furniture or an assortment of sizes) or a quantity (as a bale, gross, or carload) or any oth

perturbation.justification: The change from "batches" to "bulk" introduces ambiguity regarding the method and quantities of transportation, potentially conflicting with specific trade regulations. While Snippet 2 is about the Uniform Law Commission itself and doesn't provide specific legal context, Snippet 1, which is UCC 2-105, defines 'goods' and refers to 'lot', which implies a specific identified quantity. The change to "bulk" weakens this specificity and may lead to disputes over whether the shipment meets legal requirements. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction by misaligning the terminology with potentially more precise trade and customs regulations for crude oil exports.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Unless otherwise agreed by the Parties, the Principal shall at all times deliver to the Company Karakuduk Commodity pursuant to this Contract. The Company shall ensure that (i) during the Principal Period, the quality of Commodity delivered at the relevant delivery point shall be REBCO, and (ii) during the Secondary Period, the quality of Commodity delivered at the CPC Terminal shall be Karakuduk Commodity for segregated batch deliveries or shall be CPC Blend for deliveries for which segregated batch delivery is not available.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Unless otherwise agreed by the Parties, the Principal shall at all times deliver to the Company Karakuduk Commodity pursuant to this Contract. The Company shall ensure that (i) during the Principal Period, the quality of Commodity delivered at the relevant delivery point shall be 'good' and (ii) during the Secondary Period, the quality of Commodity delivered at the CPC Terminal shall be Karakuduk Commodity for segregated batch deliveries or shall be CPC Blend for deliveries for which segregated batch delivery is not available.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "REBCO" with "good" introduces a vague and subjective quality standard. "REBCO" has a specific technical definition (TU-39-1623-93), making quality objectively verifiable. "Good" is open to interpretation, potentially violating implied warranty laws regarding the merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. This change creates uncertainty about what quality is acceptable, making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Article 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-314 implies a warranty that goods are merchantable, meaning they meet certain minimum standards of quality. By replacing the specific 'REBCO' standard with the vague term 'good,' the contract undermines this implied warranty and could lead to disputes over whether the delivered commodity meets acceptable merchantability standards. The vagueness directly contradicts the UCC's intent to provide a clear baseline for quality in commercial transactions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! Ă No thank you Â§ 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from 'REBCO' to 'good' weakens the quality standard, creating a contradiction. UCC 2-314(2)(a) states that goods must 'pass without objection in the trade under the contract description'. Replacing a specific technical standard (REBCO) with the vague term 'good' makes it harder to determine if the goods meet this requirement, thus undermining the implied warranty of merchantability.

perturbation.original_text: 11.3 Effective legislation of the Republic of Kazakhstan shall apply to any relations of the Parties arising out of this Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.3 Mutually agreeable guidelines of the Republic of Kazakhstan shall apply to any relations of the Parties arising out of this Contract.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Effective legislation" to "Mutually agreeable guidelines" drastically alters the enforceability of the contract. "Effective legislation" clearly refers to the codified laws of Kazakhstan. "Mutually agreeable guidelines" is vague and suggests that the parties can pick and choose which laws apply, contradicting the principle that laws are binding regardless of individual agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Constitution of the Republic of Kazakhstan (specifically Article 4, which establishes the hierarchy of legal norms).
perturbation.law_citation: Constitution of the Republic of Kazakhstan, Article 4
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of the Constitution of Kazakhstan establishes the supremacy of the Constitution and laws enacted in accordance with it. By suggesting that 'mutually agreeable guidelines' can govern the parties' relationship, the modified text undermines the binding authority of Kazakh law as established by its Constitution. This clause implies that the parties can selectively adhere to legal requirements, directly contravening the constitutional framework.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "Effective legislation" with "Mutually agreeable guidelines." This undermines the binding authority of the laws of Kazakhstan. The original text clearly states that the laws of the Republic of Kazakhstan apply to the contract. The modified text introduces ambiguity by suggesting that guidelines, not necessarily laws, that are mutually agreeable, will apply. This creates a contradiction because it suggests that the parties can choose which rules to follow, rather than being bound by the laws of Kazakhstan. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly usable, the Law Explanation supports the idea that this change undermines the binding authority of Kazakh law.
